---
title: "AI Tools for Data Science and Statistics"
subtitle: "Session 1: Foundations and First Workflow"
author: "Erik Westlund, PhD"
date: "June 15, 2026"
bibliography: temp_source/references.bib
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    embed-resources: true
    incremental: true
    css: temp_source/slides.css
---

## Goals For Session 1

- Understand what LLMs can and cannot do.
- Place the current moment in historical context.
- See recurring failure modes the research keeps finding.
- Learn the working distinction between a model and a harness.
- Start using an AI-assisted workflow on a data visualization task.

## Housekeeping

- Assignments are on CoursePlus and in the GitHub
- Three assignments
  - Problem Set 1: Data Visualization
  - Problem Set 2: Statistical Model
  - Final Project

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
What do we mean by AI?
:::

::: {style="color: #ecf0f1; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center;"}
AI, machine learning, and large language models
:::

## Working Definitions

- **AI** is the broad umbrella: systems that perform tasks associated with intelligence.
- **Machine learning** estimates patterns from data and uses them to make predictions, classifications, or decisions on new cases.
- **Large language models** are systems trained to predict text and code from context.

## LLM Summary

- An LLM is a function trained to predict the next token.
- Given a sequence of tokens, it outputs a probability distribution over what comes next.
- This training objective explains the "sophisticated autocomplete" label.
- At scale, the same objective can produce behavior that *looks like* reasoning on some tasks.
- Paired with an agent, LLMs are able to complete complex tasks with only text-based prompting.

## Tokens And Embeddings

- Text is split into **tokens**: subword units, not whole words.
  - "biostatistics" $\rightarrow$ "bio" + "stat" + "istics".
- Each token is mapped to a **vector**: a list of numbers.
- These vectors capture meaning through position in high-dimensional space.

. . .

**PCA analogy:** Just as principal components analysis finds axes of variation in your data, embeddings find axes of meaning in language. Words with similar meanings cluster together.

## Attention

Core innovation [@vaswani2017attention]: **Words look at other words to determine their meaning.**

> "They walked along the **bank** of the river."

> "She deposited the check at the **bank**."

Attention resolves the difference in meaning by weighing context.

## How Do LLMs "Learn?"

Two phases:

1. **Pre-training:** Predict the next token across a massive corpus of text, code, and other artifacts. The model learns statistical patterns in the training data, encoding them in mathematical weights/parameters.
2. **Post-training / tuning:** Further train the model to follow instructions, prefer helpful responses, refuse some requests, and behave more like an assistant.

. . .

Post-training can include supervised instruction tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback, direct preference optimization, safety tuning, tool-use training, and other methods.

## Key Concepts For Users

::: {.smaller}

| Concept | What It Means |
|:--------|:--------------|
| **Context window** | How much text the model can "see" at once |
| **Temperature** | Controls randomness. Low = more deterministic, high = less deterministic |
| **Stochasticity** | Same prompt can give different answers each time |
| **No memory** | Each conversation starts from scratch unless you provide context |

:::

## What An LLM Is Not

- **Not a database.** It doesn't "look up" answers. It generates them.
- **Not a search engine.** It doesn't retrieve documents unless given tools to do so.
- **Not deterministic.** Same input $\neq$ same output.

- **Not "thinking" the way you do.** It produces both correct reasoning and convincing nonsense with no reliable way to tell the difference from the inside.
- **Not an expert.** It can sound authoritative while sometimes being correct and sometimes being completely wrong.

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
How We Will Use LLMs
:::

::: {style="color: #ecf0f1; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center;"}
Carefully!
:::

## Biostatistics: Where AI Is Effective

In my experience, AI tools can effectively:

- Write code for data cleaning, visualization, and simple to complex analyses.
- Generate boilerplate for reports and documentation (not manuscripts).
- Translate between programming languages.

## Biostatistics: What AI Can Assist

AI *can* assist in all of the following:

- Study design.
- Helping build statistical models.
- Explaining findings.
- Explain unfamiliar code or statistical concepts.

. . .

It will give you something back in all of the above contexts, but expertise is required both to verify and craft/de-sloppify into usable work.

**AI cannot take responsibility. You do.**

## Our Orientation

- Use AI for boilerplate, code scaffolding, explanations, and review.
- Give it useful context, but do not give external tools sensitive data.
- Treat generated code and interpretation as untrusted until checked.
- Use AI tools to augment your work, not to outsource judgment.

## Key Takeaways For This Course

1. **Context quality is important.** Quality outputs depend on quality inputs. Context is more than what you type.
2. **Validate everything.** You are responsible for the outputs you use.
3. **Human expertise is essential.** Developers who know code is AI-generated catch more bugs [@tang2024developer].

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
A Brief History
:::

::: {style="color: #ecf0f1; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center;"}
70 years of trying to make machines think
:::

## The Initial Vision

- **1950:** Turing proposes the imitation game. "Can machines think?"
- **1956:** Dartmouth workshop coins "Artificial Intelligence."
- **1958:** Rosenblatt builds the Perceptron, the first neural network.

. . .

The *New York Times* reports the Navy expects it to "walk, talk, see, write, reproduce itself, and be conscious."

## The Symbolic Era: GOFAI {.smaller}

"Good Old-Fashioned AI" [@haugeland1985ai] posits that intelligence is symbol manipulation.

**The approach:** Encode human knowledge as logical rules, then reason over them.

::: {.smaller}

| System | What It Did | Limitation |
|:-------|:------------|:-----------|
| ELIZA (1966) | Pattern-matched to simulate a therapist | No understanding; pure string tricks |
| SHRDLU (1971) | Understood natural language about blocks on a table | Only worked in a tiny toy world |
| MYCIN (1976) | Diagnosed bacterial infections with ~600 rules | Couldn't learn; every rule hand-written |
| CYC (1984-) | Attempted to encode all common sense | 40+ years later, still not done |

:::

## The Hype Cycle Is Not New

::: {.smaller}

| Era | The Promise | What Actually Happened |
|:----|:------------|:-----------------------|
| 1960s | "AI in a generation" | Two AI winters |
| 1980s | Expert systems will replace professionals | Too brittle; collapsed |
| 2010s | Self-driving cars by 2020 | Still not solved in 2026 |
| 2020s | AGI is imminent; all jobs automated | ??? |

:::


## AI Revival

Researchers focused on practical applications:

- **Machine learning** replaces hand-coded rules with learning from data.
- **Statistical methods** dominate: SVMs, random forests, boosting.
- **Deep learning breakthrough** (2012): AlexNet wins ImageNet by a landslide using a neural network and GPUs.

. . .

The shift: from **"encode what we know"** to **"learn from what we have."**

## AI/LLM Advancements

- **2017:** "Attention Is All You Need" introduces the Transformer architecture.
- **2018:** GPT-1. Language modeling as pre-training.
- **2020:** GPT-3. Can perform tasks from just a few examples in the prompt.
- **2021:** Codex powers GitHub Copilot.
- **2022:** ChatGPT launches.
- **2023:** GPT-4 arrives; open-source accelerates.

## Today: Reasoning Models

- **2024:** OpenAI releases o1, a "reasoning model" that thinks step-by-step.
- **2025:** o3-mini and DeepSeek-R1 push reasoning further.
- **2026:** GPT 5.5/Opus 4.8/Fable/Mythos: Frontier models grow in size and capability.

. . .

Reasoning models use the **same underlying architecture** but differences in pre- and post-training to make them more effective problem solvers.

## What Makes Reasoning Models Different?

The difference is **how they iterate**:

- A non-reasoning model answers immediately. You ask, it generates an answer.
- A reasoning model will get an answer, use a model to study its outputs, and improve upon the outputs until it's "satisfied."
- This takes longer, and costs more money, but is more accurate in most situations.

## Benchmark Progress {.smaller}

Benchmark progression, using a few relevant tests:

::: {.smaller}

| Year | Model | Math Signal | Conditions |
|:-----|:------|:------------|:-----------|
| 2021 | GPT-2 1.5B | 7% | Original MATH; math-pretrained + fine-tuned [@hendrycks2021math] |
| 2021 | GPT-3 175B | 5% | Original MATH; few-shot [@hendrycks2021math] |
| 2023 | GPT-4 | 43% | Original MATH [@fu2023cothub] |
| 2026 | GPT-5.5 | 96.4/100 | [BenchLM](https://benchlm.ai/models/gpt-5-5) math-category aggregate; not directly comparable to original MATH |
| 2026 | Claude Mythos 5 | not exposed | [BenchLM](https://benchlm.ai/models/claude-mythos-5) overall #1; restricted model, no public direct MATH score found |

:::

Model advancement is real, but take the results with a grain of salt. 

## Why Benchmark Results Aren't Everything

Even with better models, these problems are probably not solved:

- **Buggy context.** Models still don't always detect upstream bugs [@dinh2023buggy]; can propagate mistakes in analyses.
- **Poor calibration.** Models are often sometimes miscalibrated on hard questions [@phan2025hle].
- **Benchmark contamination.** Static benchmark scores can be substantially inflated under contamination [@chen2025dycodeeval].

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Current Performance And Failure Modes
:::

## LLM Failure Modes

Four patterns show up repeatedly in the literature:

- **Brittleness to context:** small upstream errors can cascade.
- **Overconfidence:** models sound certain when they are wrong.
- **Contamination:** headline benchmark scores can be inflated.
- **Distribution shift:** real-world tasks differ from curated benchmarks.

. . .

A goal for us is to develop good instincts about when the tool is likely to help, and then bring your scientific expertise to the outputs to ensure they are correct.

## Where LLMs Succeed

::: {.table-wide-first}

| Task | Performance | Source |
|:-----|:------------|:-------|
| Code generation (HumanEval, 0-shot) | 80% (GPT-4) | [@ni2024l2ceval] |
| Grade-school math (GSM8k) | 92% | [@fu2023cothub] |
| Academic knowledge (MMLU) | 86% | [@fu2023cothub] |
| Undergraduate statistics (StatEval, Statistics subset) | 87% (GPT-5) | [@lu2025stateval] |

:::

## Where LLMs Struggle {.small}

::: {.table-wide-first}

| Task | Performance | Source |
|:-----|:------------|:-------|
| Research-level statistics | ~58% (best closed model) | [@lu2025stateval] |
| Expert-level questions (HLE) | 3-13% | [@phan2025hle] |
| Real-world code translation | 8% (GPT-4) | [@pan2024lost] |
| Code completion with buggy context | 1-3% | [@dinh2023buggy] |
| Agent self-repair | 4% | [@islam2026agents-fail] |

:::

## Overall Pattern

- LLMs fail more when tasks require genuine reasoning, when context is messy, or when there's no clear template to follow.
- If your task looks like something on Stack Overflow, the model will do well.
- If it doesn't, expect more errors.

## Buggy Context: Results

::: {.smaller}

| Model | Reference Prefix | Buggy Prefix |
|:------|:-----------------|:-------------|
| CodeGen-2B | 55% | 3% |
| InCoder-6B | 51% | 1% |
| CodeGen-350M | 43% | 0.7% |
| InCoder-1B | 41% | 0.5% |

:::

- In their analysis, the model **fails to react** to the potential bug in 90% of instances [@dinh2023buggy].
- **Takeaway: Context quality is important.**
- These results are old; things have improved.

## Overconfidence

LLMs are wrong frequently and don't convey their uncertainty:

- **Wrong on expert questions:** To this day still cannot accurately pass expert-level questions across domains that are easily recalled from training data [@phan2025hle].
- On MATH, confidence is near 100% **regardless of correctness** [@hendrycks2021math].
- Bugs are common in practice; one empirical study catalogs 10 recurring bug patterns from 333 collected bugs in LLM-generated code [@tambon2024bugs].

## Benchmarks Can Lie

- **Under data contamination, static benchmark scores can jump ~4x**: for example, 22% $\rightarrow$ 82% on Pass@K [@chen2025dycodeeval].
- **Even uncontaminated benchmarks are too easy:** Code that passes HumanEval often fails on more rigorous test suites [@liu2023evalplus].
- **Be skeptical of headline numbers.**


## Making Sense of Benchmarks

- Research on benchmarks that is over a couple of years old is dated.
- Models have improved substantially over the last two years.
- Benchmarks themselves can be artificial and gamed.
- Goodhart's Law: "when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure."

. . .

Regardless of how objectively "intelligent" the models are, they still need supervision, and we still bear responsibility for how we use their outputs.


# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Harness Vs. Model
:::

::: {style="color: #ecf0f1; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center;"}
Brief version today; more detail tomorrow
:::

## Model Vs. Harness

- **Model:** the system that generates text or code from context.
- **Harness:** the environment around the model.

. . .

**Harness = Model + Tools + Permissions + Context + Workflow**

The same model with a different harness will result in different output.

## Chat Interfaces

**The standard way most people use AI:**

- Paste in some context and a question.
- Get an answer back.
- Paste the answer into your editor, fit it into your code, test.
- Repeat.

## Limits of Chat

**You act as a go-between.**

- The model has no persistent context of your files.
- It cannot test the code it writes.
- It relies entirely on your prompt clarity.

## Agents

An **agent** is a **harness** around an AI model.

Rather than just returning text, the model can:

- `read` a file in your project.
- `search` your files for context.
- `edit` files directly.
- `execute` scripts/programs to achieve tasks.

## The Agent Workflow

1. You give it a task: "Create a summary stats table for my dataset."
2. The agent explores the directory.
3. It writes the code and saves it.
4. It runs the code, reads the error log, and fixes its own mistakes.
5. It presents the final result.

## Verify Before Trust

Agents today seldom fully fail, but they make mistakes, and those mistakes can propagate.

- Syntactically valid $\neq$ statistically correct.
- Check tables and plots for accuracy.
- Use another model or another prompt to critique important output.

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.8em; text-align: center;"}
Break
:::


## Computing Check-in

- Let's pause and check in on everyone's computing setup.

## Computing Setup

Goal: get everyone to a working starting point.

- Open the course repository **as a project** in RStudio, Positron, or your preferred editor.
- Confirm `source("updater.R")` or `git pull` works.
- Confirm the data folders and assignment pages are visible.
- Use an AI tool on a small clean-summary-analysis workflow.

## Updating With R

If you use R, run this from the course project root each day to get the new material.

```r
source("updater.R")
```

What it does:

- Pulls updated course materials with Git.
- Copies missing practice-task files into `practice/work/` (this directory is `.gitignore`d).
- Leaves existing files in `practice/work/` alone.
- Saves backup copies if you accidentally edited tracked course files.

If you do not use R, run `git pull` and create your own files in `practice/work/`.

## Computing Check-Up

1. From the course project root, run `source("updater.R")`.
2. Find `practice/tasks/00_open-project-and-glimpse/`.
3. Open `open-project-and-glimpse.Rproj` in RStudio or `open-project-and-glimpse.code-workspace` in Positron.
4. Open `starter-notebook.qmd`.
5. Use the prompt in `practice/prompts/00_open-project-and-glimpse/starter-prompt.txt`.
6. Read `data/project_check.csv`, run `dplyr::glimpse()`, and render the notebook.

Stop there. This task only checks that the project root and local data paths work.

## If the Updater Fails

**If you edit files outside `practice/work/` the updater may fail.**

- Look at what files were edited; do you need any of the changes?
- If so, copy and save them.
- Run `git stash`.
- Run the updater again.

One tactic: Ask an agent to merge in new changes and preserve your work. They are very good at this.

## Note

- I am demoing in Positron, which behaves similarly to RStudio.
- Code depends on opening the right folder as a project.
- You can follow along if you do this.

## Instructor Demo

We will use the [First Agent Demo](../modules/01_agent-demo/01_data-cleaning.qmd) in the repository:

- Open only `modules/01_agent-demo/` in the agent tool.
- Data and codebook are inside that folder's `data/` directory.
- Prompts are outside the demo folder in `modules/prompts/01-agent-demo/`.
- Goal: use an AI agent to tackle an analysis task.
- We will go step by step, checking each stage before moving on.

## Workflow

1. Tell the agent about the dataset and the goal.
2. Ask it to clean the data.
3. Ask it to summarize the data.
4. Ask it to analyze the data.
5. Check the result ourselves.

## Data Visualization Practice

Open only the project folder you are working in with your agent tool.

Security reason: an agent can inspect files in the folder you give it. Do not open a larger folder than needed.

Use [Practice Task 1](../practice/tasks/01_clean-and-visualize/) or the matching copy in `practice/work/`:

- Data and codebook are inside that folder's `data/` directory.
- Prompts and instructions are outside the task folder in `practice/prompts/01_clean-and-visualize/`.
- Paste the prompt into the agent; do not put prompt files inside the project folder.
- Use the empty phase notebooks: `01_data-cleaning.qmd`, `02_data-summary.qmd`, and `03_data-analysis.qmd`.

The task folder contains the working files. The prompt/checklist folder contains instructor instructions.

## Wrap-Up

- Today: foundations, practical posture, harness vs. model, first visualization workflow.
- Tomorrow: model choice, agents, context files, structured workflows.
- Assignment: Problem Set 1 is due Thursday, June 18, 2026, at 11:59 pm.

## References
